title: Planet Labs is not selling satellite images. It is selling a subscription to watch the entire planet change in real time.
text:
Planet Labs launched three additional Pelican satellites on May 3, reaching nine spacecraft in its next-generation constellation. The company is building toward a 32-satellite fleet capable of imaging any location on Earth up to 30 times daily at 30-centimeter resolution.

The launch marks a milestone for a company that achieved profitability in late 2025, becoming the first New Space venture to reach positive EBITDA and free cash flow. In its fiscal third quarter of 2026, Planet reported revenue of $81.3 million, representing 33 percent year-on-year growth. Projected fiscal 2027 revenue of $415 million to $440 million indicates roughly 39 percent growth ahead.

Planet's business model centers on subscription-based data delivery rather than selling individual images. More than 90 per cent of revenue comes from recurring subscriptions, with gross margins expanding to 58 percent. The company maintains a $900 million contract backlog, predominantly from defense and government customers.

Major contracts include a €240 million multi-year German government agreement, renewed U.S. National Reconnaissance Office baseline contracts, a $12.8 million National Geospatial-Intelligence Agency contract, and NATO's seven-figure persistent surveillance deal.

Each Pelican satellite carries Nvidia's Jetson AI platform for onboard data processing, enabling analysis before transmission to ground stations. This technological approach reduces latency and bandwidth requirements while enhancing intelligence value for customers requiring real-time change detection.

The earth observation market was valued at approximately $7 billion in 2025 and is projected to reach $14.5 billion by 2034.
